revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]Created page with '{{draft}} [[{{PAGENAME}}]] is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. revision.text:
{{draft}} {{ill|{{PAGENAME}}|simple}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a shrine of the [[Goryo faith]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
It has a 700 year old [[shinboku]] sacred tree<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
It contains Nitta Hachimangu, an [[ichinomiya]], but itself is larger than merely that
== {{Anchor|Hachimangu}}Hachimangu == Nitta Hachimangu is a [[Hachiman shrine]] located within this shrine

It is a subshrine of Nitta Shrine

{{Short description|Shinto shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|other}}
{{draft}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = Shinto | image = Nitta-shrine (satsuma-sendai) pavilions 1.jpg | type = [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] [[Susanoo]] ([[Gozu Tenno]]) [[Nitta Yoshisada]] all the deities of its province ([[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]]) }} {{ill|{{PAGENAME}}|simple|4=ja|5=新田神社 (薩摩川内市)}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a shrine of the [[Goryo faith]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
It has a 700 year old [[shinboku]] sacred tree<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
It contains Nitta Hachimangu, an [[ichinomiya]], but itself is larger than merely that
[[Nitta Yoshisada]] is enshrined here<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine - Destinations |url=https://trip.pref.kanagawa.jp/destination/nitta-shrine/525 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=Tokyo Day Trip |language=en}}</ref> the Shrine was dedicated to him because of his death. He was perceived as having been killed in a dishonest manner, and the people who killed him are said to have died from curses. The Shrine was made to calm his soul.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index.htm |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
== {{Anchor|Hachimangu}}Hachimangu == Nitta Hachimangu is a [[Hachiman shrine]] located within this shrine
== {{Anchor|Shukō Jinja}}Shukō Jinja == {{nihongo|Shukō Jinja|九楼守公神社}} is a [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]]. It enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Satsuma Province|Satsuma]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]].
It is a subshrine of Nitta Shrine
== See Also ==
* [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Hirasaki Shrine|Hirakiki jinja]] (the other Ichinomiya of [[Satsuma Province]])

{{AFC submission|d|nn|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|decliner=NP83|declinets=20230426030233|ts=20230425021350}} <!-- Do not remove this line! -->
{{Short description|Shinto shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|other}}
{{draft}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = Shinto | image = Nitta-shrine (satsuma-sendai) pavilions 1.jpg | consecration_year = 1358 | type = [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] [[Susanoo]] ([[Gozu Tenno]]) [[Nitta Yoshisada]] all the deities of its province ([[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]]) }} {{ill|Nitta Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=新田神社 (薩摩川内市)}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a shrine of the [[Goryo]] faith.<ref name=":0" />
==History== Constructed in 1358, Nitta Shrine is a place of worship for a deity that is believed to bring family prosperity, requited love, and the fulfillment of wishes, with the maintenance of good fortune and the invitation of happiness. Upon entering the shrine grounds, which are surrounded by lush greenery, visitors will first encounter a sacred tree before proceeding to the main building at the rear. This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, Gennnai Hiraga, a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of Rangaku, a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}</ref>.
It has a 700 year old [[shinboku]] sacred tree.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref> It contains Nitta Hachimangu, an [[ichinomiya]], but itself is larger than merely that [[Nitta Yoshioki]] is enshrined here<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine - Destinations |url=https://trip.pref.kanagawa.jp/destination/nitta-shrine/525 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=Tokyo Day Trip |language=en}}</ref> the Shrine was dedicated to him because of his death. He was perceived as having been killed in a dishonest manner, and the people who killed him are said to have died from curses. The Shrine was made to calm his soul.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index.htm |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
== {{Anchor|Hachimangu}}Hachimangu == Nitta Hachimangu is a [[Hachiman shrine]] located within this shrine
== {{Anchor|Shukō Jinja}}Shukō Jinja == {{nihongo|Shukō Jinja|九楼守公神社}} is a [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]]. It enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Satsuma Province|Satsuma]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]].
It is a subshrine of Nitta Shrine

{{AFC submission|d|nn|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|decliner=NP83|declinets=20230426030233|ts=20230425021350}} <!-- Do not remove this line! -->
{{Short description|Shinto shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|other}}
{{draft}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = Shinto | image = Nitta-shrine (satsuma-sendai) pavilions 1.jpg | consecration_year = 1358 | type = [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] [[Susanoo]] ([[Gozu Tenno]]) [[Nitta Yoshisada]] all the deities of its province ([[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]]) }} '''Nitta Shrine''' (新田神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].
==History== Constructed in 1358, Nitta Shrine is a place of worship for a deity that is believed to bring family prosperity, requited love, and the fulfillment of wishes, with the maintenance of good fortune and the invitation of happiness. Upon entering the shrine grounds, which are surrounded by lush greenery, visitors will first encounter a sacred tree before proceeding to the main building at the rear. This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, Gennnai Hiraga, a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of Rangaku, a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}</ref>.
Today, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old shinboku sacred tree that has survived through numerous incredible events. Despite being struck by thunder in the Edo period, the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous samurai warrior named [[Nitta Yoshioki]], the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial Southern Court in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy. The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fiery orbs. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.
It contains Nitta Hachimangu, an [[ichinomiya]], but itself is larger than merely that [[Nitta Yoshioki]] is enshrined here<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine - Destinations |url=https://trip.pref.kanagawa.jp/destination/nitta-shrine/525 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=Tokyo Day Trip |language=en}}</ref> the Shrine was dedicated to him because of his death. He was perceived as having been killed in a dishonest manner, and the people who killed him are said to have died from curses. The Shrine was made to calm his soul.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index.htm |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
== {{Anchor|Hachimangu}}Hachimangu == Nitta Hachimangu is a [[Hachiman shrine]] located within this shrine
== {{Anchor|Shukō Jinja}}Shukō Jinja == {{nihongo|Shukō Jinja|九楼守公神社}} is a [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]]. It enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Satsuma Province|Satsuma]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]].
It is a subshrine of Nitta Shrine
== See Also ==
* [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Hirasaki Shrine|Hirakiki jinja]] (the other Ichinomiya of [[Satsuma Province]])

'''Nitta Shrine''' (新田神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. The [[ichinomiya]] [[Nitta Hachimangu]] is housed within the shrine, but the shrine holds greater significance as it enshrines Nitta Yoshioki. He was enshrined there because his death was believed to be the result of treachery, and those responsible for it were believed to have suffered a cursed fate. The shrine was established to soothe his spirit.
==History== Constructed in 1358, Nitta Shrine is a place of worship for a deity that is believed to bring family prosperity, requited love, and the fulfillment of wishes, with the maintenance of good fortune and the invitation of happiness. Upon entering the shrine grounds, which are surrounded by lush greenery, visitors will first encounter a sacred tree before proceeding to the main building at the rear. This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, Gennnai Hiraga, a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of Rangaku, a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}</ref>.
Today, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old shinboku sacred tree that has survived through numerous incredible events. Despite being struck by thunder in the Edo period, the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous samurai warrior named [[Nitta Yoshioki]], the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial Southern Court in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy. The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fiery orbs. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.
It contains Nitta Hachimangu, an [[ichinomiya]], but itself is larger than merely that [[Nitta Yoshioki]] is enshrined here<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine - Destinations |url=https://trip.pref.kanagawa.jp/destination/nitta-shrine/525 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=Tokyo Day Trip |language=en}}</ref> the Shrine was dedicated to him because of his death. He was perceived as having been killed in a dishonest manner, and the people who killed him are said to have died from curses. The Shrine was made to calm his soul.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index.htm |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>

== {{Anchor|Shukō Jinja}}Shukō Jinja == {{nihongo|Shukō Jinja|九楼守公神社}} is a [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]]. It enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Satsuma Province|Satsuma]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]].
It is a subshrine of Nitta Shrine

{{AFC submission|d|nn|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|decliner=NP83|declinets=20230426030233|ts=20230425021350}} <!-- Do not remove this line! -->
{{Short description|Shinto shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|other}}
{{draft}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = Shinto | image = Nitta-shrine (satsuma-sendai) pavilions 1.jpg | consecration_year = 1358 | type = [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] [[Susanoo]] ([[Gozu Tenno]]) [[Nitta Yoshisada]] all the deities of its province ([[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]]) }}
'''Nitta Shrine''' (新田神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. The shrine is dedicated to the memory of the valiant and wise 14th-century samurai, [[Yoshioki Nitta]]. He was enshrined there because his death was believed to have been caused by treachery, and those responsible were believed to have suffered a cursed fate. The shrine was built to calm his spirit. In addition to its historical and spiritual significance, the shrine has become a popular destination for worshippers seeking love. Although [[Nitta Hachimangu]], an [[ichinomiya]], is also housed within the shrine, its primary significance lies in enshrining Nitta Yoshioki.

During a visit to Nitta Shrine, Gennnai Hiraga, a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of Rangaku, a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}</ref>.
Today, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old shinboku sacred tree that has survived through numerous incredible events. Despite being struck by thunder in the Edo period, the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous samurai warrior named [[Nitta Yoshioki]], the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial Southern Court in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy. The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fiery orbs. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.
It contains Nitta Hachimangu, an [[ichinomiya]], but itself is larger than merely that [[Nitta Yoshioki]] is enshrined here<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine - Destinations |url=https://trip.pref.kanagawa.jp/destination/nitta-shrine/525 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=Tokyo Day Trip |language=en}}</ref> the Shrine was dedicated to him because of his death. He was perceived as having been killed in a dishonest manner, and the people who killed him are said to have died from curses. The Shrine was made to calm his soul.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index.htm |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>

== {{Anchor|Shukō Jinja}}Shukō Jinja == {{nihongo|Shukō Jinja|九楼守公神社}} is a [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]]. It enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Satsuma Province|Satsuma]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]].
It is a subshrine of Nitta Shrine

'''Nitta Shrine''' (新田神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōta, Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is dedicated to the memory of the valiant and wise 14th-century samurai, [[Nitta Yoshioki]]. He was enshrined there because his death was believed to have been caused by treachery, and those responsible were believed to have suffered a cursed fate. The shrine was built to calm his spirit. In addition to its historical and spiritual significance, the shrine has become a popular destination for worshippers seeking love. Although [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Nitta Hachimangu]], an [[ichinomiya]], is also housed within the shrine, its primary significance lies in enshrining Nitta Yoshioki.
==History== Constructed in 1358, Nitta Shrine is a place of worship for a deity that is believed to bring family prosperity, requited love, and the fulfillment of wishes, with the maintenance of good fortune and the invitation of happiness. Upon entering the shrine grounds, which are surrounded by lush greenery, visitors will first encounter a sacred tree before proceeding to the main building at the rear. This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, Gennnai Hiraga, a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of Rangaku, a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}</ref>.

This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.

The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fiery orbs. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits. It contains Nitta Hachimangu, an [[ichinomiya]], but itself is larger than merely that [[Nitta Yoshioki]] is enshrined here{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine - Destinations |url=https://trip.pref.kanagawa.jp/destination/nitta-shrine/525 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=Tokyo Day Trip |language=en}} the Shrine was dedicated to him because of his death. He was perceived as having been killed in a dishonest manner, and the people who killed him are said to have died from curses. The Shrine was made to calm his soul.{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index.htm |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=nittajinja.org}} == See Also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Hirasaki Shrine|Hirakiki jinja]] (the other Ichinomiya of [[Satsuma Province]]) == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories|[[:Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Goryō faith]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:1358 establishments]] }}

{{AFC submission|d|nn|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|decliner=NP83|declinets=20230426030233|ts=20230425021350}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|other}} {{draft}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = Shinto | image = Nitta-shrine (satsuma-sendai) pavilions 1.jpg | consecration_year = 1358 | type = [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]] | deity = [[Nitta Yoshioki]][[Nitta Yoshioki]][[Hachiman]]
[[Susanoo]] ([[Gozu Tenno]])
All the deities of its province ([[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]]) | location = [[Ōta, Tokyo]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style }} '''Nitta Shrine''' (新田神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōta, Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is dedicated to the memory of the valiant and wise 14th-century samurai, [[Nitta Yoshioki]]. He was enshrined there because his death was believed to have been caused by treachery, and those responsible were believed to have suffered a cursed fate. The shrine was built to calm his spirit. In addition to its historical and spiritual significance, the shrine has become a popular destination for worshippers seeking love. The Nitta Shrine houses both the Nitta Hachimangu, an [[ichinomiya]], and the Shukō Jinja, a [[Sōja shrine]]. ==History== Constructed in 1358, Nitta Shrine is a place of worship for a deity that is believed to bring family prosperity, requited love, and the fulfillment of wishes, with the maintenance of good fortune and the invitation of happiness. Upon entering the shrine grounds, which are surrounded by lush greenery, visitors will first encounter a sacred tree before proceeding to the main building at the rear. This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, Gennnai Hiraga, a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of Rangaku, a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}. Today, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old shinboku sacred tree that has survived through numerous incredible events. Despite being struck by thunder in the Edo period, the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}} ==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous [[samurai]] named [[Nitta Yoshioki]], the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial Southern Court in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://www.google.co.th/books/edition/An_Official_Guide_to_Eastern_Asia/1v1MAAAAYAAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21&printsec=frontcover |language=en}} The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://www.google.co.th/books/edition/Legend_in_Japanese_Art/OSxPAAAAYAAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408&printsec=frontcover |language=en}} It contains Nitta Hachimangu, an [[ichinomiya]], but itself is larger than merely that [[Nitta Yoshioki]] is enshrined here{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine - Destinations |url=https://trip.pref.kanagawa.jp/destination/nitta-shrine/525 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=Tokyo Day Trip |language=en}} the Shrine was dedicated to him because of his death. He was perceived as having been killed in a dishonest manner, and the people who killed him are said to have died from curses. The Shrine was made to calm his soul.{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index.htm |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=nittajinja.org}} == See Also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Hirasaki Shrine|Hirakiki jinja]] (the other Ichinomiya of [[Satsuma Province]]) == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories|[[:Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Goryō faith]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:1358 establishments]] }}

{{AFC submission|d|nn|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|decliner=NP83|declinets=20230426030233|ts=20230425021350}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|other}} {{draft}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = Shinto | image = | consecration_year = 1358 | type = [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]] | deity = [[Nitta Yoshioki]][[Nitta Yoshioki]][[Hachiman]]
[[Susanoo]] ([[Gozu Tenno]])
All the deities of its province ([[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]]) | location = [[Ōta, Tokyo]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style }} '''Nitta Shrine''' (新田神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōta, Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is dedicated to the memory of the valiant and wise 14th-century samurai, [[Nitta Yoshioki]]. He was enshrined there because his death was believed to have been caused by treachery, and those responsible were believed to have suffered a cursed fate. The shrine was built to calm his spirit. In addition to its historical and spiritual significance, the shrine has become a popular destination for worshippers seeking love. The Nitta Shrine houses both the Nitta Hachimangu, an [[ichinomiya]], and the Shukō Jinja, a [[Sōja shrine]].{{cite news |title=【日本の美しい禁足地vol.9】入ると恐ろしい祟りがある!?～東京都大田 |url=https://tabizine.jp/2022/07/10/478550/ |work=TABIZINE～人生に旅心を～}}{{cite news |title=新田神社（大田区）｜散歩日記 |url=https://sanpo-nikki.com/shrine/yaguchi_nittajinja/ |work=Sanpo-Nikki |language=ja}} ==History== Constructed in 1358, Nitta Shrine is a place of worship for a deity that is believed to bring family prosperity, requited love, and the fulfillment of wishes, with the maintenance of good fortune and the invitation of happiness. Upon entering the shrine grounds, which are surrounded by lush greenery, visitors will first encounter a sacred tree before proceeding to the main building at the rear. This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, Gennnai Hiraga, a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of Rangaku, a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}.{{cite news |title=運を守る神様！破魔矢の元祖〈新田神社〉で新生活の開運力アップ｜MARIKOの心潤う神社巡り |url=https://erecipe.woman.excite.co.jp/article/Hanakotokyo_371597/ |work=E・レシピ |language=ja}} Today, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old [[shinboku]] sacred tree that has survived through numerous incredible events. Despite being struck by thunder in the [[Edo period]], the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.{{cite news |title=新田神社で！女子に効く東京の初詣スポット2022 - OZmall |url=https://www.ozmall.co.jp/hatsumoude/spot/16751/ |work=オズモール |date=2 November 2022 |language=ja}}{{cite news |title=新田神社 / 東京都大田区 |url=https://jinjamemo.com/archives/nittajinja.html |work=御朱印・神社メモ |date=26 March 2023 |language=ja}}{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}} ==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous [[samurai]] named [[Nitta Yoshioki]], the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial Southern Court in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://www.google.co.th/books/edition/An_Official_Guide_to_Eastern_Asia/1v1MAAAAYAAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21&printsec=frontcover |language=en}} The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://www.google.co.th/books/edition/Legend_in_Japanese_Art/OSxPAAAAYAAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408&printsec=frontcover |language=en}} == See Also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Hirasaki Shrine|Hirakiki jinja]] (the other Ichinomiya of [[Satsuma Province]]) == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories|[[:Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Goryō faith]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:1358 establishments]] }}

The Nitta Shrine houses both the Nitta Hachimangu, an [[ichinomiya]], and the Shukō Jinja, a [[Sōja shrine]].{{cite news |title=【日本の美しい禁足地vol.9】入ると恐ろしい祟りがある!?～東京都大田 |url=https://tabizine.jp/2022/07/10/478550/ |work=TABIZINE～人生に旅心を～}}{{cite news |title=新田神社（大田区）｜散歩日記 |url=https://sanpo-nikki.com/shrine/yaguchi_nittajinja/ |work=Sanpo-Nikki |language=ja}} ==History== Constructed in 1358, Nitta Shrine is a place of worship for a deity that is believed to bring family prosperity, requited love, and the fulfillment of wishes, with the maintenance of good fortune and the invitation of happiness. Upon entering the shrine grounds, which are surrounded by lush greenery, visitors will first encounter a sacred tree before proceeding to the main building at the rear. This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, Gennnai Hiraga, a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of Rangaku, a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}{{cite news |title=運を守る神様！破魔矢の元祖〈新田神社〉で新生活の開運力アップ｜MARIKOの心潤う神社巡り |url=https://erecipe.woman.excite.co.jp/article/Hanakotokyo_371597/ |work=E・レシピ |language=ja}} Today, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old [[shinboku]] sacred tree that has survived through numerous incredible events. Despite being struck by thunder in the [[Edo period]], the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.{{cite news |title=新田神社で！女子に効く東京の初詣スポット2022 - OZmall |url=https://www.ozmall.co.jp/hatsumoude/spot/16751/ |work=オズモール |date=2 November 2022 |language=ja}}{{cite news |title=新田神社 / 東京都大田区 |url=https://jinjamemo.com/archives/nittajinja.html |work=御朱印・神社メモ |date=26 March 2023 |language=ja}}{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}} ==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous [[samurai]] named [[Nitta Yoshioki]], the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial Southern Court in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://www.google.co.th/books/edition/An_Official_Guide_to_Eastern_Asia/1v1MAAAAYAAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21&printsec=frontcover |language=en}} The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://www.google.co.th/books/edition/Legend_in_Japanese_Art/OSxPAAAAYAAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408&printsec=frontcover |language=en}} == See Also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Hirasaki Shrine|Hirakiki jinja]] (the other Ichinomiya of [[Satsuma Province]]) == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories|[[:Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Goryō faith]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:1358 establishments]] }}

This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}{{cite news |title=運を守る神様！破魔矢の元祖〈新田神社〉で新生活の開運力アップ｜MARIKOの心潤う神社巡り |url=https://erecipe.woman.excite.co.jp/article/Hanakotokyo_371597/ |work=E・レシピ |language=ja}} Today, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old [[shinboku]] sacred tree that has survived through numerous incredible events. Despite being struck by thunder in the [[Edo period]], the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.{{cite news |title=新田神社で！女子に効く東京の初詣スポット2022 - OZmall |url=https://www.ozmall.co.jp/hatsumoude/spot/16751/ |work=オズモール |date=2 November 2022 |language=ja}}{{cite news |title=新田神社 / 東京都大田区 |url=https://jinjamemo.com/archives/nittajinja.html |work=御朱印・神社メモ |date=26 March 2023 |language=ja}}{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}} [[ja:新田神社 (大田区)]] ==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous [[samurai]] named [[Nitta Yoshioki]], the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial Southern Court in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://www.google.co.th/books/edition/An_Official_Guide_to_Eastern_Asia/1v1MAAAAYAAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21&printsec=frontcover |language=en}} The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://www.google.co.th/books/edition/Legend_in_Japanese_Art/OSxPAAAAYAAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408&printsec=frontcover |language=en}} == See Also == *[[Nitta Shrine (Satsumasendai City)]] * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Hirasaki Shrine|Hirakiki jinja]] (the other Ichinomiya of [[Satsuma Province]]) == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories|[[:Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Goryō faith]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:1358 establishments]] }}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|other}} {{AfC submission|||ts=20230508214619|u=2001:44C8:4876:5FB4:5930:CF4B:2532:B535|ns=118}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = Shinto | image = Nittajinja.jpg | consecration_year = 1358 | type = [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]] | deity = [[Nitta Yoshioki]] | location = [[Ōta, Tokyo]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style }}
'''Nitta Shrine''' (新田神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōta, Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is dedicated to the memory of the valiant and wise 14th-century samurai, [[Nitta Yoshioki]]. He was enshrined there because his death was believed to have been caused by treachery, and those responsible were believed to have suffered a cursed fate. The shrine was built to calm his spirit. In addition to its historical and spiritual significance, the shrine has become a popular destination for worshippers seeking love. The Nitta Shrine houses both the Nitta Hachimangu, an [[ichinomiya]], and the Shukō Jinja, a [[Sōja shrine]].<ref>{{cite news |title=【日本の美しい禁足地vol.9】入ると恐ろしい祟りがある!?～東京都大田 |url=https://tabizine.jp/2022/07/10/478550/ |work=TABIZINE～人生に旅心を～}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社（大田区）｜散歩日記 |url=https://sanpo-nikki.com/shrine/yaguchi_nittajinja/ |work=Sanpo-Nikki |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== Constructed in 1358, Nitta Shrine is a place of worship for a deity that is believed to bring family prosperity, requited love, and the fulfillment of wishes, with the maintenance of good fortune and the invitation of happiness. Upon entering the shrine grounds, which are surrounded by lush greenery, visitors will first encounter a sacred tree before proceeding to the main building at the rear. This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, Gennnai Hiraga, a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of Rangaku, a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=運を守る神様！破魔矢の元祖〈新田神社〉で新生活の開運力アップ｜MARIKOの心潤う神社巡り |url=https://erecipe.woman.excite.co.jp/article/Hanakotokyo_371597/ |work=E・レシピ |language=ja}}</ref>
Today, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old [[shinboku]] sacred tree that has survived through numerous incredible events. Despite being struck by thunder in the [[Edo period]], the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.<ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社で！女子に効く東京の初詣スポット2022 - OZmall |url=https://www.ozmall.co.jp/hatsumoude/spot/16751/ |work=オズモール |date=2 November 2022 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社 / 東京都大田区 |url=https://jinjamemo.com/archives/nittajinja.html |work=御朱印・神社メモ |date=26 March 2023 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref> [[ja:新田神社 (大田区)]]
==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous [[samurai]] named [[Nitta Yoshioki]], the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial Southern Court in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://www.google.co.th/books/edition/An_Official_Guide_to_Eastern_Asia/1v1MAAAAYAAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21&printsec=frontcover |language=en}}</ref> The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://www.google.co.th/books/edition/Legend_in_Japanese_Art/OSxPAAAAYAAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408&printsec=frontcover |language=en}}</ref>

==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous [[samurai]] named [[Nitta Yoshioki]], the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial [[Southern Court]] in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://www.google.co.th/books/edition/An_Official_Guide_to_Eastern_Asia/1v1MAAAAYAAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21&printsec=frontcover |language=en}}</ref> The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://www.google.co.th/books/edition/Legend_in_Japanese_Art/OSxPAAAAYAAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408&printsec=frontcover |language=en}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|other}} {{AfC submission|||ts=20230508214619|u=2001:44C8:4876:5FB4:5930:CF4B:2532:B535|ns=118}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = Shinto | image = Nittajinja.jpg | consecration_year = 1358 | type = [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]] | deity = [[Nitta Yoshioki]] | location = [[Ōta, Tokyo]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style }}
'''Nitta Shrine''' (新田神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōta, Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is dedicated to the memory of the 14th-century samurai, [[Nitta Yoshioki]]. He was enshrined there because his death was believed to have been caused by treachery, and those responsible were believed to have suffered a cursed fate. The shrine was built to calm his spirit. In addition to its historical and spiritual significance, the shrine has become a popular destination for worshippers seeking love.<ref>{{cite news |title=【日本の美しい禁足地vol.9】入ると恐ろしい祟りがある!?～東京都大田 |url=https://tabizine.jp/2022/07/10/478550/ |work=TABIZINE～人生に旅心を～}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社（大田区）｜散歩日記 |url=https://sanpo-nikki.com/shrine/yaguchi_nittajinja/ |work=Sanpo-Nikki |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== Constructed in 1358, Nitta Shrine is a place of worship for a deity that is believed to bring family prosperity, requited love, and the fulfillment of wishes, with the maintenance of good fortune and the invitation of happiness. Upon entering the shrine grounds, which are surrounded by lush greenery, visitors will first encounter a sacred tree before proceeding to the main building at the rear. This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, Gennnai Hiraga, a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of Rangaku, a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=運を守る神様！破魔矢の元祖〈新田神社〉で新生活の開運力アップ｜MARIKOの心潤う神社巡り |url=https://erecipe.woman.excite.co.jp/article/Hanakotokyo_371597/ |work=E・レシピ |language=ja}}</ref>
Today, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old [[shinboku]] sacred tree that has survived through numerous incredible events. Despite being struck by thunder in the [[Edo period]], the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.<ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社で！女子に効く東京の初詣スポット2022 - OZmall |url=https://www.ozmall.co.jp/hatsumoude/spot/16751/ |work=オズモール |date=2 November 2022 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社 / 東京都大田区 |url=https://jinjamemo.com/archives/nittajinja.html |work=御朱印・神社メモ |date=26 March 2023 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref> [[ja:新田神社 (大田区)]]
==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous [[samurai]] named [[Nitta Yoshioki]], the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial [[Southern Court]] in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://www.google.co.th/books/edition/An_Official_Guide_to_Eastern_Asia/1v1MAAAAYAAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21&printsec=frontcover |language=en}}</ref> The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://www.google.co.th/books/edition/Legend_in_Japanese_Art/OSxPAAAAYAAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408&printsec=frontcover |language=en}}</ref>

This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://www.google.co.th/books/edition/Legend_in_Japanese_Art/OSxPAAAAYAAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408&printsec=frontcover |language=en}}</ref>

revision.comment: CurryTime7-24 moved page [[Draft:Nitta Shrine (Ōta City)]] to [[Nitta Shrine (Ōta City)]]: Publishing accepted [[Wikipedia:Articles for creation|Articles for creation]] submission ([[WP:AFCH|AFCH]] 0.9.1)
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|other}} {{AfC submission|||ts=20230508214619|u=2001:44C8:4876:5FB4:5930:CF4B:2532:B535|ns=118}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = Shinto | image = Nittajinja.jpg | consecration_year = 1358 | type = [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]] | deity = [[Nitta Yoshioki]] | location = [[Ōta, Tokyo]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style }}
'''Nitta Shrine''' (新田神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōta, Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is dedicated to the memory of the 14th-century samurai, [[Nitta Yoshioki]]. He was enshrined there because his death was believed to have been caused by treachery, and those responsible were believed to have suffered a cursed fate. The shrine was built to calm his spirit. In addition to its historical and spiritual significance, the shrine has become a popular destination for worshippers seeking love.<ref>{{cite news |title=【日本の美しい禁足地vol.9】入ると恐ろしい祟りがある!?～東京都大田 |url=https://tabizine.jp/2022/07/10/478550/ |work=TABIZINE～人生に旅心を～}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社（大田区）｜散歩日記 |url=https://sanpo-nikki.com/shrine/yaguchi_nittajinja/ |work=Sanpo-Nikki |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== Constructed in 1358, Nitta Shrine is a place of worship for a deity that is believed to bring family prosperity, requited love, and the fulfillment of wishes, with the maintenance of good fortune and the invitation of happiness. Upon entering the shrine grounds, which are surrounded by lush greenery, visitors will first encounter a sacred tree before proceeding to the main building at the rear. This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, [[Hiraga Gennai]], a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of [[Rangaku]], a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=運を守る神様！破魔矢の元祖〈新田神社〉で新生活の開運力アップ｜MARIKOの心潤う神社巡り |url=https://erecipe.woman.excite.co.jp/article/Hanakotokyo_371597/ |work=E・レシピ |language=ja}}</ref>
Nowadays, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old [[shinboku]] sacred tree that has survived through numerous incredible events. Despite being struck by thunder in the [[Edo period]], the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.<ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社で！女子に効く東京の初詣スポット2022 - OZmall |url=https://www.ozmall.co.jp/hatsumoude/spot/16751/ |work=オズモール |date=2 November 2022 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社 / 東京都大田区 |url=https://jinjamemo.com/archives/nittajinja.html |work=御朱印・神社メモ |date=26 March 2023 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref> [[ja:新田神社 (大田区)]]
==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous [[samurai]] named [[Nitta Yoshioki]], the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial [[Southern Court]] in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://www.google.co.th/books/edition/An_Official_Guide_to_Eastern_Asia/1v1MAAAAYAAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21&printsec=frontcover |language=en}}</ref> The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://www.google.co.th/books/edition/Legend_in_Japanese_Art/OSxPAAAAYAAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408&printsec=frontcover |language=en}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine}}
{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = Shinto | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Nittajinja.jpg | consecration_year = 1358 | type = [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]] | deity = [[Nitta Yoshioki]] | location = [[Ōta, Tokyo]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style }}
'''Nitta Shrine''' (新田神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōta, Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is dedicated to the memory of the 14th-century samurai, [[Nitta Yoshioki]]. He was enshrined there because his death was believed to have been caused by treachery, and those responsible were believed to have suffered a cursed fate. The shrine was built to calm his spirit. In addition to its historical and spiritual significance, the shrine has become a popular destination for worshippers seeking love.<ref>{{cite news |title=【日本の美しい禁足地vol.9】入ると恐ろしい祟りがある!?～東京都大田 |url=https://tabizine.jp/2022/07/10/478550/ |work=TABIZINE～人生に旅心を～}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社（大田区）｜散歩日記 |url=https://sanpo-nikki.com/shrine/yaguchi_nittajinja/ |work=Sanpo-Nikki |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== Constructed in 1358, Nitta Shrine is a place of worship for a deity that is believed to bring family prosperity, requited love, and the fulfillment of wishes, with the maintenance of good fortune and the invitation of happiness. Upon entering the shrine grounds, which are surrounded by lush greenery, visitors will first encounter a sacred tree before proceeding to the main building at the rear. This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, [[Hiraga Gennai]], a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of [[Rangaku]], a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=運を守る神様！破魔矢の元祖〈新田神社〉で新生活の開運力アップ｜MARIKOの心潤う神社巡り |url=https://erecipe.woman.excite.co.jp/article/Hanakotokyo_371597/ |work=E・レシピ |language=ja}}</ref>
Nowadays, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old [[shinboku]] sacred tree that has survived through numerous events. Despite being struck by thunder in the [[Edo period]], the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.<ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社で！女子に効く東京の初詣スポット2022 - OZmall |url=https://www.ozmall.co.jp/hatsumoude/spot/16751/ |work=オズモール |date=2 November 2022 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社 / 東京都大田区 |url=https://jinjamemo.com/archives/nittajinja.html |work=御朱印・神社メモ |date=26 March 2023 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous [[samurai]] named [[Nitta Yoshioki]], the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial [[Southern Court]] in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1v1MAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21 |language=en}}</ref> The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OSxPAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408 |language=en}}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Nitta Shrine (Satsumasendai City)|Nitta Shrine]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine}}
{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = Shinto | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Nittajinja.jpg | consecration_year = 1358 | type = [[Sōja shrine|Sōja shrine]] | deity = [[Nitta Yoshioki]] | location = [[Ōta, Tokyo]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style }}
'''Nitta Shrine''' (新田神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōta, Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is dedicated to the memory of the 14th-century samurai, [[Nitta Yoshioki]]. He was enshrined there because his death was believed to have been caused by treachery, and those responsible were believed to have suffered a cursed fate. The shrine was built to calm his spirit. In addition to its historical and spiritual significance, the shrine has become a popular destination for worshippers seeking love.<ref>{{cite news |title=【日本の美しい禁足地vol.9】入ると恐ろしい祟りがある!?～東京都大田 |url=https://tabizine.jp/2022/07/10/478550/ |work=TABIZINE～人生に旅心を～}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社（大田区）｜散歩日記 |url=https://sanpo-nikki.com/shrine/yaguchi_nittajinja/ |work=Sanpo-Nikki |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== Constructed in 1358, Nitta Shrine is a place of worship for a deity that is believed to bring family prosperity, requited love, and the fulfillment of wishes, with the maintenance of good fortune and the invitation of happiness. Upon entering the shrine grounds, which are surrounded by lush greenery, visitors will first encounter a sacred tree before proceeding to the main building at the rear. This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, [[Hiraga Gennai]], a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of [[Rangaku]], a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=運を守る神様！破魔矢の元祖〈新田神社〉で新生活の開運力アップ｜MARIKOの心潤う神社巡り |url=https://erecipe.woman.excite.co.jp/article/Hanakotokyo_371597/ |work=E・レシピ |language=ja}}</ref>
Nowadays, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old [[shinboku]] sacred tree that has survived through numerous events. Despite being struck by thunder in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.<ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社で！女子に効く東京の初詣スポット2022 - OZmall |url=https://www.ozmall.co.jp/hatsumoude/spot/16751/ |work=オズモール |date=2 November 2022 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社 / 東京都大田区 |url=https://jinjamemo.com/archives/nittajinja.html |work=御朱印・神社メモ |date=26 March 2023 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous [[samurai]] named [[Nitta Yoshioki]], the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial [[Southern Court]] in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1v1MAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21 |language=en}}</ref> The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OSxPAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408 |language=en}}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Nitta Shrine (Satsumasendai City)|Nitta Shrine]]

Lane |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OSxPAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408 |language=en}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Nitta Shrine (Ōta)]][[ja:新田神社 (大田区)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q22119884]] Wikidata name: Nitta Shrine (Ōta City) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine}}
{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = Shinto | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Nittajinja.jpg | consecration_year = 1358 | type = [[Sōja shrine|Sōja shrine]] | deity = [[:en:Nitta Yoshioki|Nitta Yoshioki]] | location = [[:en:Ōta, Tokyo|Ōta, Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style | prefecture= {{ill|Ota|WD=Q217234}} | coordinates= {{Coord|35.5658083|N|139.6913361|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Nitta Shrine''' (新田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ōta, Tokyo|Ōta, Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is dedicated to the memory of the 14th-century samurai, [[:en:Nitta Yoshioki|Nitta Yoshioki]]. He was enshrined there because his death was believed to have been caused by treachery, and those responsible were believed to have suffered a cursed fate. The shrine was built to calm his spirit. In addition to its historical and spiritual significance, the shrine has become a popular destination for worshippers seeking love.<ref>{{cite news |title=【日本の美しい禁足地vol.9】入ると恐ろしい祟りがある!?～東京都大田 |url=https://tabizine.jp/2022/07/10/478550/ |work=TABIZINE～人生に旅心を～}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社（大田区）｜散歩日記 |url=https://sanpo-nikki.com/shrine/yaguchi_nittajinja/ |work=Sanpo-Nikki |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== Constructed in 1358, Nitta Shrine is a place of worship for a deity that is believed to bring family prosperity, requited love, and the fulfillment of wishes, with the maintenance of good fortune and the invitation of happiness. Upon entering the shrine grounds, which are surrounded by lush greenery, visitors will first encounter a sacred tree before proceeding to the main building at the rear. This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, [[:en:Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]], a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of [[:en:Rangaku|Rangaku]], a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=運を守る神様！破魔矢の元祖〈新田神社〉で新生活の開運力アップ｜MARIKOの心潤う神社巡り |url=https://erecipe.woman.excite.co.jp/article/Hanakotokyo_371597/ |work=E・レシピ |language=ja}}</ref>
Nowadays, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old [[shinboku]] sacred tree that has survived through numerous events. Despite being struck by thunder in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.<ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社で！女子に効く東京の初詣スポット2022 - OZmall |url=https://www.ozmall.co.jp/hatsumoude/spot/16751/ |work=オズモール |date=2 November 2022 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社 / 東京都大田区 |url=https://jinjamemo.com/archives/nittajinja.html |work=御朱印・神社メモ |date=26 March 2023 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] named [[:en:Nitta Yoshioki|Nitta Yoshioki]], the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial [[Southern Court]] in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1v1MAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21 |language=en}}</ref> The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OSxPAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408 |language=en}}</ref>
