Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1v1MAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21 |language=en}}</ref> The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OSxPAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408 |language=en}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Nitta Shrine (Ōta)]][[ja:新田神社 (大田区)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q22119884]] Wikidata name: Nitta Shrine (Ōta City) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine}}
{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = Shinto | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Nittajinja.jpg | consecration_year = 1358 | type = [[Sōja shrine|Sōja shrine]] | deity = [[:en:Nitta Yoshioki|Nitta Yoshioki]] | location = [[:en:Ōta, Tokyo|Ōta, Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style | prefecture= {{ill|Ota|WD=Q217234}} | coordinates= {{Coord|35.5658083|N|139.6913361|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Nitta Shrine''' (新田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ōta, Tokyo|Ōta, Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is dedicated to the memory of the 14th-century samurai, [[:en:Nitta Yoshioki|Nitta Yoshioki]]. He was enshrined there because his death was believed to have been caused by treachery, and those responsible were believed to have suffered a cursed fate. The shrine was built to calm his spirit. In addition to its historical and spiritual significance, the shrine has become a popular destination for worshippers seeking love.<ref>{{cite news |title=【日本の美しい禁足地vol.9】入ると恐ろしい祟りがある!?～東京都大田 |url=https://tabizine.jp/2022/07/10/478550/ |work=TABIZINE～人生に旅心を～}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社（大田区）｜散歩日記 |url=https://sanpo-nikki.com/shrine/yaguchi_nittajinja/ |work=Sanpo-Nikki |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== Constructed in 1358, Nitta Shrine is a place of worship for a deity that is believed to bring family prosperity, requited love, and the fulfillment of wishes, with the maintenance of good fortune and the invitation of happiness. Upon entering the shrine grounds, which are surrounded by lush greenery, visitors will first encounter a sacred tree before proceeding to the main building at the rear. This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, [[:en:Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]], a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of [[:en:Rangaku|Rangaku]], a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=運を守る神様！破魔矢の元祖〈新田神社〉で新生活の開運力アップ｜MARIKOの心潤う神社巡り |url=https://erecipe.woman.excite.co.jp/article/Hanakotokyo_371597/ |work=E・レシピ |language=ja}}</ref>
Nowadays, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old [[shinboku]] sacred tree that has survived through numerous events. Despite being struck by thunder in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.<ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社で！女子に効く東京の初詣スポット2022 - OZmall |url=https://www.ozmall.co.jp/hatsumoude/spot/16751/ |work=オズモール |date=2 November 2022 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社 / 東京都大田区 |url=https://jinjamemo.com/archives/nittajinja.html |work=御朱印・神社メモ |date=26 March 2023 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] named [[:en:Nitta Yoshioki|Nitta Yoshioki]], the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial [[Southern Court]] in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1v1MAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21 |language=en}}</ref> The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OSxPAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408 |language=en}}</ref>

This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, [[:en:Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]], a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of [[:en:Rangaku|Rangaku]], a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=運を守る神様！破魔矢の元祖〈新田神社〉で新生活の開運力アップ｜MARIKOの心潤う神社巡り |url=https://erecipe.woman.excite.co.jp/article/Hanakotokyo_371597/ |work=E・レシピ |language=ja}}</ref>
Nowadays, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old [[shinboku]] sacred tree that has survived through numerous events. Despite being struck by thunder in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.<ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社で！女子に効く東京の初詣スポット2022 - OZmall |url=https://www.ozmall.co.jp/hatsumoude/spot/16751/ |work=オズモール |date=2 November 2022 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社 / 東京都大田区 |url=https://jinjamemo.com/archives/nittajinja.html |work=御朱印・神社メモ |date=26 March 2023 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] named [[:en:Nitta Yoshioki|Nitta Yoshioki]], the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial [[Southern Court]] in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1v1MAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21 |language=en}}</ref> The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OSxPAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408 |language=en}}</ref>

'''Nitta Shrine''' (新田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ōta, Tokyo|Ōta, Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is dedicated to the memory of the 14th-century samurai, [[:en:Nitta Yoshioki|Nitta Yoshioki]]. He was enshrined there because his death was believed to have been caused by treachery, and those responsible were believed to have suffered a cursed fate. The shrine was built to calm his spirit. In addition to its historical and spiritual significance, the shrine has become a popular destination for worshippers seeking love.<ref>{{cite news |title=【日本の美しい禁足地vol.9】入ると恐ろしい祟りがある!?～東京都大田 |url=https://tabizine.jp/2022/07/10/478550/ |work=TABIZINE～人生に旅心を～}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社（大田区）｜散歩日記 |url=https://sanpo-nikki.com/shrine/yaguchi_nittajinja/ |work=Sanpo-Nikki |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== Constructed in 1358, Nitta Shrine is a place of worship for a deity that is believed to bring family prosperity, requited love, and the fulfillment of wishes, with the maintenance of good fortune and the invitation of happiness. Upon entering the shrine grounds, which are surrounded by lush greenery, visitors will first encounter a sacred tree before proceeding to the main building at the rear. This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, [[:en:Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]], a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of [[:en:Rangaku|Rangaku]], a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=運を守る神様！破魔矢の元祖〈新田神社〉で新生活の開運力アップ｜MARIKOの心潤う神社巡り |url=https://erecipe.woman.excite.co.jp/article/Hanakotokyo_371597/ |work=E・レシピ |language=ja}}</ref>
Nowadays, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old [[shinboku]] sacred tree that has survived through numerous events. Despite being struck by thunder in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.<ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社で！女子に効く東京の初詣スポット2022 - OZmall |url=https://www.ozmall.co.jp/hatsumoude/spot/16751/ |work=オズモール |date=2 November 2022 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社 / 東京都大田区 |url=https://jinjamemo.com/archives/nittajinja.html |work=御朱印・神社メモ |date=26 March 2023 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] named [[:en:Nitta Yoshioki|Nitta Yoshioki]], the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial [[Southern Court]] in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1v1MAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21 |language=en}}</ref> The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = Shinto | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Nittajinja.jpg | consecration_year = 1358 | type = [[Sōja shrine|Sōja shrine]] | deity = [[:en:Nitta Yoshioki|Nitta Yoshioki]] | location = [[:en:Ōta, Tokyo|Ōta, Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style | prefecture= {{ill|Ota|WD=Q217234}} | coordinates= {{Coord|35.5658083|N|139.6913361|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Nitta Shrine''' (新田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ōta, Tokyo|Ōta, Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is dedicated to the memory of the 14th-century samurai, [[:en:Nitta Yoshioki|Nitta Yoshioki]]. He was enshrined there because his death was believed to have been caused by treachery, and those responsible were believed to have suffered a cursed fate. The shrine was built to calm his spirit. In addition to its historical and spiritual significance, the shrine has become a popular destination for worshippers seeking love.<ref>{{cite news |title=【日本の美しい禁足地vol.9】入ると恐ろしい祟りがある!?～東京都大田 |url=https://tabizine.jp/2022/07/10/478550/ |work=TABIZINE～人生に旅心を～}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社（大田区）｜散歩日記 |url=https://sanpo-nikki.com/shrine/yaguchi_nittajinja/ |work=Sanpo-Nikki |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== Constructed in 1358, Nitta Shrine is a place of worship for a deity that is believed to bring family prosperity, requited love, and the fulfillment of wishes, with the maintenance of good fortune and the invitation of happiness. Upon entering the shrine grounds, which are surrounded by lush greenery, visitors will first encounter a sacred tree before proceeding to the main building at the rear. This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, [[:en:Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]], a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of [[:en:Rangaku|Rangaku]], a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=運を守る神様！破魔矢の元祖〈新田神社〉で新生活の開運力アップ｜MARIKOの心潤う神社巡り |url=https://erecipe.woman.excite.co.jp/article/Hanakotokyo_371597/ |work=E・レシピ |language=ja}}</ref>
Nowadays, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old [[shinboku]] sacred tree that has survived through numerous events. Despite being struck by thunder in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.<ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社で！女子に効く東京の初詣スポット2022 - OZmall |url=https://www.ozmall.co.jp/hatsumoude/spot/16751/ |work=オズモール |date=2 November 2022 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社 / 東京都大田区 |url=https://jinjamemo.com/archives/nittajinja.html |work=御朱印・神社メモ |date=26 March 2023 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] named [[:en:Nitta Yoshioki|Nitta Yoshioki]], the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial [[Southern Court]] in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1v1MAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21 |language=en}}</ref> The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OSxPAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408 |language=en}}</ref>
== See also ==

The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old [[shinboku]] sacred tree that has survived through numerous events. Despite being struck by thunder in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.<ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社で！女子に効く東京の初詣スポット2022 - OZmall |url=https://www.ozmall.co.jp/hatsumoude/spot/16751/ |work=オズモール |date=2 November 2022 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社 / 東京都大田区 |url=https://jinjamemo.com/archives/nittajinja.html |work=御朱印・神社メモ |date=26 March 2023 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} named {{ill|Nitta Yoshioki|en|Nitta Yoshioki|WD=Q3342234|lt=Nitta Yoshioki}}, the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial [[Southern Court]] in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1v1MAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21 |language=en}}</ref> The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OSxPAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408 |language=en}}</ref>

{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = Shinto | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Nittajinja.jpg | consecration_year = 1358 | type = [[Sōja shrine|Sōja shrine]] | deity = {{ill|Nitta Yoshioki|en|Nitta Yoshioki|WD=Q3342234|lt=Nitta Yoshioki}} | location = {{ill|Ōta, Tokyo|en|Ōta, Tokyo|WD=Q217234|lt=Ōta, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style | prefecture= {{ill|Ota|WD=Q217234}} | coordinates= {{Coord|35.5658083|N|139.6913361|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Nitta Shrine''' (新田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Ōta, Tokyo|en|Ōta, Tokyo|WD=Q217234|lt=Ōta, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The shrine is dedicated to the memory of the 14th-century samurai, {{ill|Nitta Yoshioki|en|Nitta Yoshioki|WD=Q3342234|lt=Nitta Yoshioki}}. He was enshrined there because his death was believed to have been caused by treachery, and those responsible were believed to have suffered a cursed fate. The shrine was built to calm his spirit. In addition to its historical and spiritual significance, the shrine has become a popular destination for worshippers seeking love.<ref>{{cite news |title=【日本の美しい禁足地vol.9】入ると恐ろしい祟りがある!?～東京都大田 |url=https://tabizine.jp/2022/07/10/478550/ |work=TABIZINE～人生に旅心を～}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社（大田区）｜散歩日記 |url=https://sanpo-nikki.com/shrine/yaguchi_nittajinja/ |work=Sanpo-Nikki |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== Constructed in 1358, Nitta Shrine is a place of worship for a deity that is believed to bring family prosperity, requited love, and the fulfillment of wishes, with the maintenance of good fortune and the invitation of happiness. Upon entering the shrine grounds, which are surrounded by lush greenery, visitors will first encounter a sacred tree before proceeding to the main building at the rear. This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, {{ill|Hiraga Gennai|en|Hiraga Gennai|WD=Q968242|lt=Hiraga Gennai}}, a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of {{ill|Rangaku|en|Rangaku|WD=Q877637|lt=Rangaku}}, a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=運を守る神様！破魔矢の元祖〈新田神社〉で新生活の開運力アップ｜MARIKOの心潤う神社巡り |url=https://erecipe.woman.excite.co.jp/article/Hanakotokyo_371597/ |work=E・レシピ |language=ja}}</ref>
Nowadays, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old [[shinboku]] sacred tree that has survived through numerous events. Despite being struck by thunder in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.<ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社で！女子に効く東京の初詣スポット2022 - OZmall |url=https://www.ozmall.co.jp/hatsumoude/spot/16751/ |work=オズモール |date=2 November 2022 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社 / 東京都大田区 |url=https://jinjamemo.com/archives/nittajinja.html |work=御朱印・神社メモ |date=26 March 2023 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} named {{ill|Nitta Yoshioki|en|Nitta Yoshioki|WD=Q3342234|lt=Nitta Yoshioki}}, the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial [[Southern Court]] in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1v1MAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21 |language=en}}</ref> The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OSxPAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408 |language=en}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine}}
{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = Shinto | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Nittajinja.jpg | consecration_year = 1358 | type = [[Sōja shrine|Sōja shrine]] | deity = {{ill|Nitta Yoshioki|en|Nitta Yoshioki|WD=Q3342234|lt=Nitta Yoshioki}} | location = {{ill|Ōta, Tokyo|en|Ōta, Tokyo|WD=Q217234|lt=Ōta, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style | prefecture= {{ill|Ota|WD=Q217234}} | coordinates= {{Coord|35.5658083|N|139.6913361|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Nitta Shrine''' (新田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Ōta, Tokyo|en|Ōta, Tokyo|WD=Q217234|lt=Ōta, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The shrine is dedicated to the memory of the 14th-century samurai, {{ill|Nitta Yoshioki|en|Nitta Yoshioki|WD=Q3342234|lt=Nitta Yoshioki}}. He was enshrined there because his death was believed to have been caused by treachery, and those responsible were believed to have suffered a cursed fate. The shrine was built to calm his spirit. In addition to its historical and spiritual significance, the shrine has become a popular destination for worshippers seeking love.<ref>{{cite news |title=【日本の美しい禁足地vol.9】入ると恐ろしい祟りがある!?～東京都大田 |url=https://tabizine.jp/2022/07/10/478550/ |work=TABIZINE～人生に旅心を～}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社（大田区）｜散歩日記 |url=https://sanpo-nikki.com/shrine/yaguchi_nittajinja/ |work=Sanpo-Nikki |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== Constructed in 1358, Nitta Shrine is a place of worship for a deity that is believed to bring family prosperity, requited love, and the fulfillment of wishes, with the maintenance of good fortune and the invitation of happiness. Upon entering the shrine grounds, which are surrounded by lush greenery, visitors will first encounter a sacred tree before proceeding to the main building at the rear. This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, {{ill|Hiraga Gennai|en|Hiraga Gennai|WD=Q968242|lt=Hiraga Gennai}}, a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of {{ill|Rangaku|en|Rangaku|WD=Q877637|lt=Rangaku}}, a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=運を守る神様！破魔矢の元祖〈新田神社〉で新生活の開運力アップ｜MARIKOの心潤う神社巡り |url=https://erecipe.woman.excite.co.jp/article/Hanakotokyo_371597/ |work=E・レシピ |language=ja}}</ref>
Nowadays, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old [[shinboku]] sacred tree that has survived through numerous events. Despite being struck by thunder in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.<ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社で！女子に効く東京の初詣スポット2022 - OZmall |url=https://www.ozmall.co.jp/hatsumoude/spot/16751/ |work=オズモール |date=2 November 2022 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社 / 東京都大田区 |url=https://jinjamemo.com/archives/nittajinja.html |work=御朱印・神社メモ |date=26 March 2023 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} named {{ill|Nitta Yoshioki|en|Nitta Yoshioki|WD=Q3342234|lt=Nitta Yoshioki}}, the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial [[Southern Court]] in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1v1MAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21 |language=en}}</ref> The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OSxPAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408 |language=en}}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Nitta Shrine (Satsumasendai City)|Nitta Shrine]]

The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old [[shinboku]] sacred tree that has survived through numerous events. Despite being struck by thunder in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.<ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社で！女子に効く東京の初詣スポット2022 - OZmall |url=https://www.ozmall.co.jp/hatsumoude/spot/16751/ |work=オズモール |date=2 November 2022 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社 / 東京都大田区 |url=https://jinjamemo.com/archives/nittajinja.html |work=御朱印・神社メモ |date=26 March 2023 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} named {{ill|Nitta Yoshioki|en|Nitta Yoshioki|WD=Q3342234|lt=Nitta Yoshioki|1=Nitta Yoshioki}}, the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial [[Southern Court]] in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1v1MAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21 |language=en}}</ref> The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OSxPAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408 |language=en}}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Nitta Shrine (Satsumasendai City)|Nitta Shrine]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Nitta Shrine (Ōta)]][[ja:新田神社 (大田区)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Nitta Shrine (Ōta)]][[ja:新田神社 (大田区)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Nitta Shrine (Ōta)]][[ja:新田神社 (大田区)]]
{{Short description|Shinto shrine}}
{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = Shinto | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Nittajinja.jpg | consecration_year = 1358 | type = [[Sōja shrine|Sōja shrine]] | deity = {{ill|Nitta Yoshioki|en|Nitta Yoshioki|WD=Q3342234|lt=Nitta Yoshioki|1=Nitta Yoshioki}} | location = {{ill|Ōta, Tokyo|en|Ōta, Tokyo|WD=Q217234|lt=Ōta, Tokyo|1=Ōta, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style | prefecture= {{ill|Ota|WD=Q217234|1=Ōta, Tokyo}} | coordinates= {{Coord|35.5658083|N|139.6913361|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Nitta Shrine''' (新田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Ōta, Tokyo|en|Ōta, Tokyo|WD=Q217234|lt=Ōta, Tokyo|1=Ōta, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The shrine is dedicated to the memory of the 14th-century samurai, {{ill|Nitta Yoshioki|en|Nitta Yoshioki|WD=Q3342234|lt=Nitta Yoshioki|1=Nitta Yoshioki}}. He was enshrined there because his death was believed to have been caused by treachery, and those responsible were believed to have suffered a cursed fate. The shrine was built to calm his spirit. In addition to its historical and spiritual significance, the shrine has become a popular destination for worshippers seeking love.<ref>{{cite news |title=【日本の美しい禁足地vol.9】入ると恐ろしい祟りがある!?～東京都大田 |url=https://tabizine.jp/2022/07/10/478550/ |work=TABIZINE～人生に旅心を～}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社（大田区）｜散歩日記 |url=https://sanpo-nikki.com/shrine/yaguchi_nittajinja/ |work=Sanpo-Nikki |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== Constructed in 1358, Nitta Shrine is a place of worship for a deity that is believed to bring family prosperity, requited love, and the fulfillment of wishes, with the maintenance of good fortune and the invitation of happiness. Upon entering the shrine grounds, which are surrounded by lush greenery, visitors will first encounter a sacred tree before proceeding to the main building at the rear. This shrine is also renowned as the birthplace of hamaya, an arrow used for warding off evil spirits. During a visit to Nitta Shrine, {{ill|Hiraga Gennai|en|Hiraga Gennai|WD=Q968242|lt=Hiraga Gennai|1=Hiraga Gennai}}, a pharmacist from the Edo period who was also a student of {{ill|Rangaku|en|Rangaku|WD=Q877637|lt=Rangaku|1=Rangaku}}, a body of knowledge that was developed through Japan's limited association with Dutch culture and knowledge, created "yamori," an arrow made from the mysterious bamboo grown within the shrine's precincts, and recommended that people deify and believe in it. This is the origin of the current hamaya, which is believed to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine (Haneda{{!}}Shrines) |url=https://livejapan.com/en/in-tokyo/in-pref-tokyo/in-haneda/spot-lj0002278/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=LIVE JAPAN |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=運を守る神様！破魔矢の元祖〈新田神社〉で新生活の開運力アップ｜MARIKOの心潤う神社巡り |url=https://erecipe.woman.excite.co.jp/article/Hanakotokyo_371597/ |work=E・レシピ |language=ja}}</ref>
Nowadays, the Nitta Shrine is a revered and popular destination for those seeking good luck, and it commemorated its 650th anniversary in 2008. The shrine's reputation as a place of good fortune has made it a significant cultural and spiritual landmark in Japan. In the shrine's premises, there stands a 700-year-old [[shinboku]] sacred tree that has survived through numerous events. Despite being struck by thunder in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the tree remained standing, and during the Tokyo air raid, the entire area around Nitta Shrine was burned down, leaving only the Zelkova tree intact.<ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社で！女子に効く東京の初詣スポット2022 - OZmall |url=https://www.ozmall.co.jp/hatsumoude/spot/16751/ |work=オズモール |date=2 November 2022 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=新田神社 / 東京都大田区 |url=https://jinjamemo.com/archives/nittajinja.html |work=御朱印・神社メモ |date=26 March 2023 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Nitta Shrine -English site- |url=https://nittajinja.org/nitta_english/index2.htm |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=nittajinja.org}}</ref>
==Belief== During the Nanboku-cho period, a famous {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} named {{ill|Nitta Yoshioki|en|Nitta Yoshioki|WD=Q3342234|lt=Nitta Yoshioki|1=Nitta Yoshioki}}, the second son of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], fought for the Imperial [[Southern Court]] in Japan's North-South divide. Yoshioki was known for his bravery and strategic wisdom, and his death in a cowardly attack at the Yaguchi Ferry marked the end of his legacy.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Tetsudōshō |first1=Japan |title=An Official Guide to Eastern Asia: Trans-continental Connections Between Europe and Asia ... |date=1914 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1v1MAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA21 |language=en}}</ref> The samurai warriors who were responsible for Yoshioki's death were believed to have been cursed by his vengeful spirit, which caused them to experience terrifying ghostly phenomena that included lightning strikes and fire balls. To appease Yoshioki's spirit, local villagers constructed a shrine at the site of his death, based on the Japanese belief of "Goryo Shinko," which involved enshrining the spirits of deceased individuals who could protect people from harm. This belief was widely held across Japan and helped to mitigate the fear of natural disasters and plagues that were often attributed to evil spirits.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Joly |first1=Henri L. |title=Legend in Japanese Art: A Description of Historical Episodes, Legendary Characters, Folk-lore, Myths, Religious Symbolism, Illustrated in the Arts of Old Japan |date=1908 |publisher=J. Lane |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OSxPAAAAYAAJ&dq=Nitta+Shrine++Nitta+Yoshioki&pg=PA408 |language=en}}</ref>
